note:
“以前”似乎是十年前的事了，不是吗？那些拥抱朋友和握手的无忧无虑的日子并没有被禁止，在离开家之前，我们不需要伸手拿面罩，也不需要寻找洗手液的补给。哦，那是在社交距离不是我们方言的一部分的日子里。
然而，事实上，距离世界了解到一种新的危险冠状病毒在我们中间只有七个月的时间。自从中国科学家确认武汉迅速传播的疾病是由一种新的冠状病毒引起并将其基因序列公布在网上以来，人们对这种名为SARS-CoV-2的病毒及其引起的疾病新冠肺炎以及它们如何影响我们的了解非常多。
以下是我们学到的一些东西，以及一些我们仍然需要回答的紧迫问题。
在大流行的早期，席卷世界的疾病云看起来有一线希望。孩子们似乎没有出现成年人呼吸困难时会被送往医院的严重症状，而且他们很少死亡。甚至看起来，孩子们感染这种疾病的比率并不像成年人那样高。
但Covid的一切都很复杂，孩子们也不例外。虽然儿童和青少年的死亡率仍然很低，但他们并不是坚不可摧的。它们可能对SARS-CoV-2的传播有贡献，尽管具体有多少还不清楚。
我们已经了解到，当涉及到Covid时，年龄较小的儿童和青少年不应该被混为一谈。青少年似乎以与成年人大致相同的速度排出病毒-从他们的喉咙和鼻腔排出病毒。5岁以下的儿童呼吸道病毒水平很高，但目前还不清楚他们传播了多少病毒，也不清楚为什么他们不像成年人那样经常出现症状。
佐治亚州一个露宿营地最近发布的一份报告显示，病毒在儿童中传播的速度有多快。营地不得不在开始对营地工作人员进行迎新的10天内关闭，因为孩子们到达后的几天内，孩子和工作人员就开始生病。(露营者的年龄从6岁到19岁不等。)。营地没有要求露营者戴口罩。
美国疾病控制和预防中心(Centers For Disease Control And Prevention)最近发布的一份关于儿童Covid感染的报告显示，虽然儿童Covid感染的比例仍然很低，但自疫情开始以来，美国新冠肺炎在儿童中的住院率有所上升。每三个患有这种疾病的儿童中就有一个最终被送进重症监护病房。儿童住院率最高的是2岁以下儿童。
黑人和拉丁裔儿童的住院率高于白人儿童。和成年人一样，患有其他健康状况的儿童-肥胖、慢性肺部疾病或早产婴儿-比其他健康儿童面临更高的风险。
也许最令人担忧的是，很明显，感染新冠肺炎的儿童中有一小部分会继续发展成多个器官受到自身免疫系统攻击的情况。这种情况被称为儿童多系统炎症综合征或MISC，似乎发生在新冠肺炎感染后大约两到四周。大多数患上这种综合症的儿童都会康复。
关于什么类型的环境会增加感染者将病毒传染给其他人的风险，研究已经在几个关键点上达成一致。
从本质上讲，你与有传染性的人关系越密切，与他们接触的时间越长，你感染病毒的可能性就越大，这有助于解释为什么这么多的传播发生在家庭中。呆在室内更糟糕，特别是在通风不充分的房间里；空气流动越多，病毒稀释的速度就越快。每天的面罩可以减少投射的病毒数量，但并不能完全封锁病毒。
大声说话、沉重的呼吸、歌唱和尖叫会排出更多的病毒，这就是为什么专家指出夜总会和健身房是危险的营业场所。(这并不是说，在与外面的人进行安静的交谈时不可能感染病毒-只是可能性较小。)。
与某人长时间、近距离接触的风险更高的部分原因是，你需要接触的病毒水平有一个临界值才能被感染。(稍后将详细介绍这一点。)。此外，一些人为什么病得这么重的一个假设是，他们暴露在更高“剂量”的病毒中。
研究人员还发现，一些相对较小比例的感染者-可能是10%到20%-推动了大约80%的新病例，通常是通过酒吧、肉类加工厂和家庭等室内场所的“超级传播”事件。是否会发生这种传播取决于一系列变量：一个地方有多少人，房间里的通风情况如何，当然还有有没有传染新冠肺炎的人在那里。一些人可能比其他人排出更多的病毒，人们或多或少有可能在感染的不同时间点传播病毒。有证据表明，在出现症状的人开始感到不适之前，传染性就会激增。
人们可以在康复后很长一段时间内检测呈阳性。无所谓
几个月前，一些看似从新冠肺炎感染中恢复过来的人几周来病毒检测继续呈阳性，这让人非常担忧。他们有传染性吗？是否应该改变对感染者应该隔离多长时间的建议？
事实证明，这是一个测试问题。大多数检测都是使用一种名为PCR(聚合酶链式反应)的平台进行的，该平台寻找SARS-CoV-2病毒的微小片段。但是测试不能确定这些遗传密码片段是可以感染其他人的实际病毒的一部分，还是绝对没有威胁的病毒片段。
现在很明显，有轻微或不复杂感染的人在症状开始后的某个地方会排出活跃的病毒长达10天。(香港大学冠状病毒专家马利克·佩里斯(Malik Peiris)说，“重病或免疫功能低下的患者确实会排出传染性病毒的时间更长。”)。
一周又一周的阳性检测-就像魁北克的这名妇女在55天内不能拥抱她的婴儿儿子-并没有告诉我们这些人仍然对其他人构成风险。世界卫生组织头号冠状病毒专家Maria Van Kerkhove说：“事实上，我们知道它们的传染性不会持续那么长时间。”
说出身体部位或系统的名字，新冠肺炎就会在那里留下指纹。我们知道这一点：异常粘性的血液会堵塞通往心脏以及感染者大脑和肺部内部的血管，导致心脏病发作、中风和致命的肺栓塞。越来越多的人担心，这些和其他健康影响将是长期的。
心脏：德国的心脏核磁共振显示，病毒引发的免疫反应的高炎症会严重削弱心肌，即使是轻度感染的年轻人也可能面临未来心力衰竭的风险。更直接的是，当他们从感染中恢复时，一些人会感到胸痛或感觉到他们的心跳在加速。大学运动员也不例外。
大脑：首次出现新冠肺炎症状的人可能已经失去了嗅觉和味觉，可能会发现他们的嗅觉缺失仍然存在。头痛和头晕很常见。感染之后，焦虑、抑郁和创伤后应激障碍(PTSD)等情绪障碍随之而来，被称为“Covid雾”的精神混乱让人们搜索词语，努力学习简单的数学知识，或者干脆试着思考。
周围神经系统：在意大利，三名新冠肺炎患者经历了重症肌无力，这是一种自身免疫性疾病，可能是由于脱髓鞘所致。脱髓鞘，即神经细胞的保护层受到免疫系统的攻击，会导致虚弱、麻木和刺痛。在某些情况下，它会刺激精神病和幻觉。一些患者患有格林-巴利综合征，这是一种罕见的自身免疫性疾病，会干扰神经信号，导致异常感觉、虚弱，有时还会瘫痪。
他们有名字，在社交媒体上的存在越来越多，但也有一个问题。他们是“长途运输者”，他们在新冠肺炎的感染中幸存下来，但感觉离正常还有很长一段路要走。我们知道他们在那里，但我们不知道有多少人，为什么他们的症状持续存在，以及接下来会发生什么。
7月份，疾控中心进行的一项调查发现，在SARS-CoV-2检测呈阳性并有新冠肺炎症状(咳嗽、疲劳或呼吸急促)但没有住院的人中，有35%的人在两到三周后没有恢复到原来的健康状态。在18岁到34岁之间没有慢性病的人中，20%的人感到疾病的迹象延长了。
国家心肺血液研究所启动了一项观察性研究，追踪新冠肺炎的长期疗效，目标是在50家医院出院后6个月跟踪3,000名患者。
纽约市的西奈山卫生系统在5月份开设了一个冷藏后护理中心，以治疗长途运输者。那里的康复创新总监大卫·普特里诺(David Putrino)表示，自主神经失调-当心率、血压和体温脱节-可能是长期令人痛苦的症状的罪魁祸首。新冠肺炎为什么会导致这一点不得而知，也不是最好的治疗方法。
尽管中国和俄罗斯已经为部分测试的疫苗颁发了紧急使用许可证，但世界上仍然没有被证明对新冠肺炎具有保护作用的疫苗。
但在创纪录的时间内，新冠肺炎疫苗取得了非同寻常的进展。试验被压缩和重叠，制造商在某些情况下进行1/2期试验，在其他情况下进行2/3期试验。
与此同时，他们一直在扩大生产能力，以便能够生产数亿剂疫苗，甚至在发现他们的候选疫苗是否真的有效之前就已经开始生产了。这项工作是在各国政府、比尔和梅林达·盖茨基金会以及CEPI(防疫创新联盟)的大量财政支持下完成的。
这就是所谓的“风险”生产--这个词很贴切。如果这些疫苗中的一些不起作用，那么产出的疫苗将不得不被丢弃。但是，如果第三阶段试验显示它们确实有效，那么一旦食品和药物管理局(Food And Drug Administration)或另一个国家的监管机构批准了这些疫苗中的任何一种，就可以开始部署。
这意味着，在发现新病毒大约一年后，就可以开始接种完全经过批准的疫苗。这构成了疫苗开发的一场革命。
讨论新冠肺炎的无症状病例，不由自主地提出了一些令人头疼的语义问题。有些人在感染期间确实没有症状，但这个词也经常被用来描述那些症状前的人-那些将会出现症状但还没有的人。其他人没有典型的新冠肺炎症状-发烧、咳嗽、嗅觉丧失-只是感觉一天有点糟糕。它们适合放在哪里？
无论你谈论的是什么群体，都有一些对大流行的关键影响，并试图控制它。其一：一些百分比的感染者-根据最近的一项审查，大约20%，尽管其他研究得出了更高的估计-根本没有表现出症状。第二：无论某人是否没有症状或无症状，他们仍然可以传播病毒(尽管他们是否像有症状的人一样有效地传播病毒仍不得而知)。这就是为什么公共卫生运动一直在强调疏远、口罩和手部卫生，而不仅仅是那些感到不适的人。一旦你开始出现症状，并试图限制与他人的接触，就太晚了，无法阻止传播。
与其他病毒家族相比，冠状病毒一般不会变异得很快。这是一件好事：例如，领先的候选疫苗是基于SARS-CoV-2的基因序列，所以从理论上讲，该阵容的重大变化可能会阻碍任何疫苗的有效性。到目前为止，这似乎还没有发生。
尽管如此，科学家们还是注意到了基因组中较小的变化。最受关注的是组成病毒RNA的“字母”的一次交换，这创造了“G变种”。这一转变发生在大流行的早期，自那以后，G变种在世界各地占据主导地位。然而，科学家们还不能弄清楚，G变种是否比它的前身更具竞争力--也许它更具传染性？--或者这只是一种偶然。到目前为止，他们还没有证据表明感染G变种的人比感染另一种变种的人或多或少会生病。这可能只是一种变异，就像把你的T恤从海军蓝变成皇家蓝一样-这是一种审美上的差异，但也是相当中性的东西。
整个春天，你几乎找不到洗手液。担心潜伏在表面的病毒会让我们感染新冠肺炎，我们中的大多数人都变成了狂热的表面清洁工。一些人甚至隔离了他们的邮件，在等待潜在的挥之不去的病毒死亡的过程中，他们几天都没有碰过它。
现在的普遍共识是，“菌群”-表面的细菌-不是新冠肺炎的主要传播途径。世卫组织的范·科克霍夫表示，目前还没有记录到明显有人单独感染混合菌属的病例。
(在现实世界中，与感染者有足够密切接触而被感染的人很可能会在表面上遇到病毒，以及携带病毒的飞沫，甚至可能是含有病毒的气溶胶大小的小颗粒，这些颗粒已经通过咳嗽、唱歌或说话被排出体外。梳理出哪些传播途径触发了感染可能是不可能做到的。)
但从大量研究中可以清楚地看出，感染者周围的表面可能会被病毒污染，病毒可能会持续存在。公共卫生官员同意，清洁表面和谨慎对待手部卫生是人们可以采取的降低风险的步骤。
人们的想法是，新冠肺炎的病例和其他感染一样，会在一段时间内对再次感染产生一定的免疫力。但研究人员不会确切知道这种保护能持续多久，直到人们再次开始接受新冠肺炎治疗。
所有这些都支持这样的观点，即新冠肺炎的行为与其他病毒感染一样，包括其他冠状病毒引起的疾病。研究人员发现，大多数感染者都会产生一种免疫反应，包括抗体和免疫细胞，这种免疫反应会清除病毒，并会持续一段时间。抗体水平下降的报道引发了一些担忧，即对SARS-CoV-2的保护可能不会持续很长时间，这对所需疫苗接种的频率有很大影响。但免疫学家指出，其他病毒的抗体也会减弱；它们的水平在再次接触病原体时会激增，但它们仍然可以阻止再次感染。
研究人员不确定需要什么水平的抗体来阻止病毒在细胞中站稳脚跟，以及抗击病原体的T细胞在抵御感染方面可能起到什么作用。从新冠肺炎康复的人也会产生不同水平的抗体-可能产生较弱初始免疫反应的人可能不会在很长一段时间内免受再次感染的保护。
“我们不知道这种免疫反应能持续多久，”世卫组织的范克霍夫上周说。“我们不知道它有多强.”
鉴于大多数呼吸道病毒不是“一劳永逸”的感染--它们不会像麻疹那样引发终身免疫--人们很有可能不止一次感染新冠肺炎。
人类冠状病毒的经验支持这一观点，冠状病毒主要导致感冒。荷兰的一项研究对人们进行了数十年的跟踪调查，定期测量他们对四种人类冠状病毒的抗体，并寻找可能表明新感染的变化。科学家们发现，再次感染可能发生在第一次感染后的一年内。(这项研究是一份预印本，这意味着它还没有通过同行评议程序。)。
一些科学家推测，在随后的感染中，免疫系统对新冠肺炎的反应可能会更快，导致较温和的感染。如果这是真的，那么随着时间的推移，SARS-CoV-2病毒可能会转变为一个不那么令人望而生畏的威胁。但这仍然是一个很大的未知数。“我们不知道，”范克霍夫说。“我不想揣测.”
当你遇到病原体时，你是否被感染不仅仅是你是否易感或免疫的问题。这取决于你遇到多少病毒(或细菌)。
能够扭转这一平衡的量就是众所周知的最小感染量。有些病原体的感染量很低。例如，不需要大量的大肠杆菌0157(一种在食物中传播的危险细菌)就能使人生病。
需要多大剂量的SARS-CoV-2才能感染大多数人？哥伦比亚大学冠状病毒专家安吉拉·拉斯穆森(Angela Rasmussen)说，这是SARS-CoV-2研究中亟待解决的问题之一。“我们不知道引起感染所需的量，但看起来可能不是像麻疹那样非常非常小的量。”
全球已有2,100万例新冠肺炎确诊病例，美国有5,300万例。比这多得多的人实际上感染了这种病毒。
检测方面的问题和有限的可获得性，以及一些人有如此轻微的症状或没有症状，以至于他们不知道自己被感染的事实，也是造成这种差距的原因之一。但是研究人员不知道他们正在处理的鸿沟到底有多大--他们错过了多少传播。
“血清学”--依赖于检测社区中SARS-CoV-2抗体的水平--正开始帮助填补一些知识。美国疾病控制与预防中心(CDC)最近对10个城市和州进行的一项研究估计，在大多数地方，真实的感染人数大约是确诊病例数量的10倍。
尽管如此，可能还有20%的人，即使是在受灾严重的社区，也有可能对新冠肺炎免疫。这意味着，即使在遭受严重疫情爆发的地区，群体免疫力仍遥遥无期--在这一点上，许多人都具有免疫力，以至于病毒不能传播。
不清楚为什么有些人会得重病，有些人不会
患上新冠肺炎的人的结局范围之广-从真正的无症状病例，到轻微的症状，到中度疾病导致长达数月的并发症，直到死亡-让传染病研究人员感到困惑。
对于谁面临更高的严重疾病风险，有一些明显的因素：老年人，以及患有从癌症到肥胖症再到镰状细胞疾病的各种疾病的人。
但科学家们推测，许多其他潜在的因素可能有助于解释为什么大多数健康的30岁的人在几天后就摆脱了病毒，有些人病得很严重。研究人员正在研究患者的基因差异，而其他人则在研究血型。
最近的研究指出了另一个潜在的参与者。也许多达一半的人拥有免疫系统T细胞，这些T细胞最初是为了应对一种常见的引起感冒的冠状病毒的感染而产生的，但它也可以识别SARS-CoV-2。这些“交叉反应”的T细胞可以帮助增强免疫系统，以延缓严重的症状，但研究人员并不确定它们到底起到了什么作用，如果真的起作用的话。
note_en:
The “before times” seem like a decade ago, don’t they? Those carefree days when hugging friends and shaking hands wasn’t verboten, when we didn’t have to reach for a mask before leaving our homes, or forage for supplies of hand sanitizer. Oh, for the days when social distancing wasn’t part of our vernacular.
 In reality, though, it’s only been about seven months since the world learned a new and dangerous coronavirus was in our midst. In the time since Chinese scientists confirmed the rapidly spreading disease in Wuhan was caused by a new coronavirus and posted its genetic sequence on line, an extraordinary amount has been learned about the virus, SARS-CoV-2, the disease it causes, Covid-19, and how they affect us.
 Here are some of the things we have learned, and some of the pressing questions we still need answered.
      Early in the pandemic, it looked like there was a silver lining to the disease cloud sweeping across the world. Children, it seemed, didn’t develop the severe symptoms that were sending adults to hospitals struggling for breath, and they very rarely died. It even seemed that kids didn’t contract the disease at the same rates as adults did.
   But everything Covid is complex, and kids are no exception. While deaths among children and teens remain low, they are not invulnerable. And they probably contribute to transmission of SARS-CoV-2, though how much remains unclear.
 We’ve learned younger children and teenagers shouldn’t be lumped together when it comes to Covid. Teens seem to shed virus — emit it from their throats and nasal passages — at about the same rates as adults. Kids under 5 have high levels of virus in their respiratory tracts, but it’s still not clear how much they spread it or why they don’t develop symptoms as often as adults do.
 A recently  published report from a Georgia sleep-away camp shows how quickly the virus can spread among kids. The camp had to be closed within 10 days of starting its orientation for camp staffers, because within days of children arriving, kids and staff started getting sick. (The campers ranged in age from 6 to 19.) The camp did not require campers to wear face masks.
   A  recent report on Covid infections in children from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention showed that while they remain low, U.S. hospitalization rates for Covid-19 in children have risen since the pandemic started. And one in three children hospitalized with the disease ends up in intensive care. The highest rate of hospitalizations in children was among those under 2 years of age.
 Black and Latino children were hospitalized at higher rates than white children. And like adults, children with other health conditions — obesity, chronic lung diseases, or infants who were born premature — are at higher risk than otherwise healthy children.
 Perhaps most alarmingly, it’s become clear that a small proportion of children infected with Covid-19 go on to develop a condition where multiple organs come under attack from their own immune system. Called multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children or  MIS-C, this condition seems to occur about two to four weeks after Covid-19 infection. Most children who develop this syndrome recover.
   Research has coalesced on a few key points about what types of setting increase the risk that an infectious person will pass the virus to others.
 Essentially, the closer you are to someone infectious and the longer you’re in contact with them, the more likely you are to contract the virus, which helps explain why  so much transmission occurs within households. Being indoors is worse, particularly in rooms without sufficient ventilation; the more air flow, the faster the virus gets diluted. Everyday face coverings reduce the amount of virus projected, but aren’t total blockades.
   Loud talking, heavy breathing, singing, and screaming expel more virus, which is why experts point to nightclubs and gyms as risky businesses to be open. (That’s not to say it’s impossible to catch the virus while having a quiet conversation with someone outside — it’s just less likely.)
 The reason having prolonged, proximate contact with someone is riskier is in part because there is a threshold level of virus you need to be exposed to to become infected. (More on this later.) Also, one hypothesis for why some people get so sick is that they are exposed to higher “doses” of virus.
 Researchers are also finding that some relatively small proportion of infected people — maybe 10% to 20% — are driving some 80% of new cases, often through “superspreading” events in indoor settings like bars, meat processing plants, and homes. Whether such transmission occurs depends on a host of variables: how many people are in a given place, what the ventilation in the room is like, and, of course, whether someone with infectious Covid-19 is there. Some people might shed more virus than others, and people are more or less likely to spread the virus during different points in their infection. Evidence suggests that contagiousness spikes in the days before people who will go on to show symptoms start feeling sick.
  People can test positive for a long time after they recover. It doesn’t matter
 There was a lot of angst a few months ago about some people who had seemingly recovered from Covid-19 infections continuing to test positive for the virus for weeks. Were they infectious? Should recommendations be changed for how long infected people should be isolated?
 It turns out it is an issue of testing. Most testing is conducted using a platform called PCR — polymerase chain reaction — that looks for tiny fragments of the SARS-CoV-2 virus. But the test can’t tell if those sections of genetic code are part of actual viruses that can infect someone else, or fragments of viruses that are absolutely no threat.
 It’s clear now that people who had mild or uncomplicated infections shed active virus for somewhere up to 10 days after their symptoms started. (“Severely ill or immunocompromised patients do shed infectious virus for longer,” said Malik Peiris, a coronavirus expert at Hong Kong University.)
 The weeks and weeks of positive tests — like those that prevented  this woman in Quebec from cuddling her infant son for 55 days — don’t tell us that these people are still a risk to others. “In fact, we know that they are not infectious for that long,” said Maria Van Kerkhove, the World Health Organization’s leading coronavirus expert.
     Name a body part or system and Covid-19 has  left its fingerprints there. We know this: Unusually sticky blood can clog vessels on the way to the heart and inside the brain and lungs of infected people, causing heart attacks, strokes, and deadly pulmonary embolisms. There are growing worries that these and other health effects will be long-lasting.
 Heart: The hyperinflammation of an immune response triggered by the virus can weaken heart muscles so much that even young people who had mild infections may be at risk for future heart failure,  cardiac MRIs in Germany indicate. More immediately, some people have  chest pain or feel like their hearts are racing as they recover from the infection. And  college athletes are no exception
 Brain: People whose first Covid-19 symptom might have been losing their sense of smell and taste may find their  anosmia persists. Headaches and dizziness are common. Mood disorders such as anxiety, depression, and PTSD follow in the wake of infection, and the  mental confusion called “Covid fog” leaves people searching for words, struggling with simple math, or simply trying to think.
 Peripheral nervous system: In Italy,  three Covid-19 patients experienced myasthenia gravis, an autoimmune disorder, possibly due to demyelination. Demyelination, in which the protective coating of nerve cells is attacked by the immune system, can cause weakness, numbness, and tingling. In some cases it can spur psychosis and hallucinations. Some patients have Guillain-Barre syndrome, a rare autoimmune disease that interferes with nerve signals, leading to abnormal sensations, weakness, and sometimes paralysis.
      They have a name, a growing  social media presence, and a problem. They are the “long-haulers,” people who have survived their Covid-19 infections but feel a long way from normal. We know they’re out there, but we don’t know how many, why their symptoms persist, and what happens next.
 In July, a  survey conducted by the CDC found that 35% of people who tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 and had symptoms of Covid-19 — cough, fatigue, or shortness of breath — but were not hospitalized had not returned to their previous health two to three weeks later. Among those between 18 and 34 years old who had no previous chronic conditions, 20% felt prolonged signs of illness.
 The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute has launched an observational  study to track the long-term effects of Covid-19, aiming to follow 3,000 patients six months after being discharged from 50 hospitals.
 Mount Sinai Health System in New York City opened a  Center for Post-Covid Care in May to treat long-haulers. David Putrino, director of rehabilitation innovation there, has suggested dysautonomia — when heart rate, blood pressure, and body temperature are disjointed —could be to blame for prolonged and distressing symptoms. Why Covid-19 would cause this isn’t known, nor is the best treatment.
   The world still doesn’t have a vaccine that has been shown to be protective against Covid-19, though China and Russia have issued emergency use licenses for partially tested vaccines.
 But an extraordinary amount of progress toward Covid-19 vaccines has been made, in record time. Trials have been  compressed and overlapped, with manufacturers running Phase 1/2 trials in some cases and Phase 2/3 trials in others.
   Meanwhile, they’ve been building out production capacity to be able to produce hundreds of millions of doses and have started production, even before finding out whether their vaccine candidate actually works. This work is being done with substantial financial support of governments, the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, and CEPI, the Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations.
 It’s called “at risk” production — and the term is apt. If some of these vaccines don’t work, that output will have to be junked. But if Phase 3 trials show they do work, deployment could begin as soon as the Food and Drug Administration, or a regulator in another country, approves any of these vaccines.
 That means vaccination with fully approved vaccines could begin as soon as about a year after the discovery of the new virus. This constitutes a revolution in vaccine development.
   Discussing asymptomatic cases of Covid-19 automatically raises some headache-inducing semantic issues. Some people are truly asymptomatic throughout their infections, but the word is often also used to describe people who are presymptomatic — those who will show symptoms but haven’t yet. Other people don’t show classic Covid-19 symptoms — fever, cough, loss of smell — but just feel kinda crappy for a day. Where do they fit in?
 Whatever group you’re talking about, there are some key implications for the pandemic, and trying to rein it in. One: Some percentage of infected people — roughly 20%, according to  one recent review, though other studies have produced higher estimates — do not show symptoms at all. And two: Whether or not someone is asymptomatic or presymptomatic, they can still spread the virus (though whether they spread it as efficiently as people with symptoms is still unknown). That is why public health campaigns have been stressing distancing, masks, and hand hygiene for everyone, not just people who feel sick. Once you do start showing symptoms and try to restrict contact with others, it is too late to prevent spread.
   Coronaviruses in general do not mutate very quickly compared to other viral families. This is a good thing: The leading vaccine candidates, for example, are based on SARS-CoV-2’s genetic sequence, so theoretically a major change in that lineup could hinder the effectiveness of any vaccine. So far, that doesn’t seem to have happened.
 Still, scientists have noticed smaller changes in the genome. The one that has gotten the most attention was one swap in the “letters” that make up the virus’ RNA, which created the “G variant.” The switch happened early in the pandemic, and the G variant has since become dominant around the world. Scientists haven’t been able to figure out, however, whether the G variant is outcompeting its predecessor — perhaps it’s more contagious? — or if that’s just chance. And so far, they haven’t landed on evidence that people who contract the G variant get more or less sick than those infected by the other variant. It could just be a mutation that’s like changing your T-shirt from navy blue to royal blue — an aesthetic difference, but something pretty neutral.
   Throughout the spring, you could barely find hand sanitizer. Fears that viruses lurking on surfaces could infect us with Covid-19 turned most of us into zealous surface cleaners. Some people went so far as to quarantine their mail, not touching it for days as they waited for potential lingering viruses to die.
 The general consensus now is that “fomites” — germs on surfaces — aren’t the major transmission route for Covid-19. Van Kerkhove of WHO said there hasn’t been a case recorded where it’s clear someone was infected by fomites alone.
 (In the real world, someone in close enough contact with an infected person to become infected will likely have encountered viruses on surfaces and virus-laced droplets and maybe even small, aerosol-sized particles containing the virus that have been expelled by coughing, singing, or speaking. Teasing out of that situation which route of transmission triggered infection may be impossible to do.)
 But it’s clear from lots of studies that surfaces around infected people can be contaminated with viruses and the viruses can linger. Cleaning surfaces and being prudent about hand hygiene is a risk-lowering step people can take, public health officials agree.
    The thinking is that a case of Covid-19, like other infections, will confer some immunity against reinfection for some amount of time. But researchers won’t know exactly how long that protection lasts until people start getting Covid-19 again.
  All that supports the notion that Covid-19 acts like other viral infections, including illnesses caused by other coronaviruses. Researchers are finding that most infected people mount an immune response involving both antibodies and immune cells that clears the virus, and that persists for some amount of time. Reports of waning antibody levels incited some concern that perhaps protection to SARS-CoV-2 might not last very long, with big implications for the frequency of required vaccine boosts. But immunologists have  pointed out that antibodies for other viruses wane as well; their levels surge upon re-exposure to the pathogen and they can still halt reinfection.
   Researchers don’t know for sure what level of antibodies are required to block the virus from gaining a toehold in cells, and what role pathogen-fighting T cells might have in fending off an infection. People who recover from Covid-19 also produce varying levels of antibodies — it’s possible people who generate a weaker initial immune response might not be protected for as long from reinfection.
 “We don’t know for how long that immune response lasts,” the WHO’s Van Kerkhove said last week. “We don’t know how strong it is.”
   Given that most respiratory viruses are not “one-and-done” infections — they don’t induce life-long immunity in the way a virus like measles does — there is a reasonable chance that people could have more than one infection with Covid-19.
 Experience with human coronaviruses — which mostly cause colds — supports that idea. A  study in the Netherlands followed people for decades, measuring their antibodies to four human coronaviruses at regular intervals and looking for changes that would indicate a new infection. The scientists found that reinfection could occur within a year of the first infection. (The study is a preprint, meaning it hasn’t yet been through the peer review process.)
 Some scientists  have theorized that on subsequent infections, immune systems might mount quicker responses to Covid-19, leading to milder infections. If that’s true, the SARS-CoV-2 virus might transition into a less daunting threat over time. But it’s still a big unknown. “We don’t know,” Van Kerkhove said. “I don’t want to speculate.”
   Whether you become infected or not when you encounter a pathogen isn’t just a question of whether you’re susceptible or immune. It depends on how much of the virus (or bacterium) you encounter.
 And the amount capable of tipping the balance is what’s known as the minimum infectious dose. Some pathogens have a low infectious dose. For example, it doesn’t take a lot of E. coli 0157, a dangerous bacterium transmitted in food, to make someone sick.
 How big a dose of SARS-CoV-2 does it take to infect most people? It’s one of the burning questions in SARS-CoV-2 research, said Angela Rasmussen, a coronavirus expert at Columbia University. “We don’t know the amount that is required to cause an infection, but it seems that it’s probably not a really, really small amount, like measles.”
   There have been 21 million confirmed cases of Covid-19 around the world, and 5.3 million in the United States. Far more people than that have actually had the virus.
 Problems with testing, and its limited availability, have contributed to that gap, as has the fact that some people have such mild or no symptoms that they don’t know they’re infected. But researchers don’t know just how big of a gulf they’re dealing with — how much spread they’ve missed.
 “Serosurveys” — which rely on testing for the level of SARS-CoV-2 antibodies in a community — are starting to help fill in some knowledge.  A recent CDC study of 10 cities and states estimated that in most places, the true number of infections was some 10 times higher than the number of confirmed cases.
 Still, that leaves perhaps 20% of people, even in hard-hit communities, with potential immunity to Covid-19. That means that herd immunity — the point at which so many people are immune that the virus can’t circulate — remains far off even in areas that have suffered severe outbreaks.
  It’s not clear why some people get really sick, and some don’t
 The sheer range of outcomes for people who get Covid-19 — from a truly asymptomatic case, to mild symptoms, to moderate disease leading to months-long complications, to death — has befuddled infectious disease researchers.
 There are some clear factors for who faces higher risks of getting severely ill: older people, as well as people  with conditions ranging from cancer to obesity to sickle cell disease.
 But scientists have postulated that a host of other underlying factors could help dictate why most healthy 30-year-olds shake off the virus after a couple days and some get severely ill. Researchers are studying genetic differences in patients, while others are looking at blood type.
 Recent studies have pointed to another potential player. Perhaps up to half the population has immune-system T cells that were initially generated in response to an infection by one of the common cold-causing coronaviruses but that can recognize SARS-CoV-2 as well. These “cross-reactive” T cells could help give the immune system the boost it needs to stave off serious symptoms, but researchers don’t know for sure what role, if any, they actually play.
title: 七个月后，我们对新冠肺炎的了解-以及仍然存在的问题
title_en: Seven months later, what we know about Covid-19 – and the questions that remain

note:
起初，很难想象透明墙壁的公共厕所如何可能帮助缓解厕所焦虑-但日本最具创新精神的建筑师之一的一项违反直觉的设计就是为了做到这一点。
在世界各地，公共厕所受到了恶毒的指责。甚至在日本，那里的厕所比世界上其他许多地方的卫生标准都要高，居民们也担心公共厕所又黑又脏，臭气熏天，令人毛骨悚然。
为治愈公众恐惧症，非营利性组织日本基金会发起了“东京厕所计划”，委托16位知名建筑师对位于涉谷公园的17座公厕进行改造，涉谷是东京最繁忙的商业区之一。
根据日本基金会的一份声明，该项目的使命是应用创新的设计，使每个人都可以使用公共厕所，不分性别、年龄或残疾，目标是“让人们在使用这些公共厕所时感到舒适，并培养对下一个人的好客精神”。
到目前为止，谈论最多的设计来自普利兹克奖获得者坂茂(Shigeru Ban)，他的透明卫生间本月在Haru-no-Ogawa社区公园和福町迷你公园(Yoyogi Fukamachi Mini Park)如雨后春笋般涌现。
这两个单元各有三个隔间，周围环绕着青色、石灰绿、蓝色、黄色、粉色或紫色的透明有色玻璃。透明设计有一个实用的原因，那就是让人在进入之前可以很容易地检查里面。
非营利组织日本基金会解释说：“公厕有两个问题，特别是那些位于公园里的公厕。”“一是里面干净不干净，二是里面没有人偷偷等着.”
该设计依赖于一项新的SmartGlass技术，当门被锁住时，该技术可以使墙壁变得不透明。根据日本基金会的说法，“在晚上，他们像一盏美丽的灯笼一样点亮公园。”
除了潘基文设计的两个设施外，“东京厕所项目”还开设了另外三个公共厕所，分别是室内设计师片山正一(Masamichi Katayama)在惠比苏公园设计的；普利兹克(Pritzker)获奖者Mumihiko Maki在惠比苏东公园设计的；以及纽约家具设计师田村直(Nao Tamura)在惠比苏车站附近设计的。
在接下来的几周里，建筑师Takenosuke Sakakura在西原Itchome公园和另一位普利兹克奖获得者安藤忠雄(Tadao Ando)将在靖国道公园开放卫生间。该项目的其余翻修工程计划于2021年春季开放。
note_en:
At first, it’s hard to fathom how a public restroom with transparent walls could possibly help ease toilet anxiety — but a counterintuitive design by one of Japan’s most innovative architects aims to do just that.
  Around the world, public toilets get a foul rap. Even in Japan, where restrooms have a higher standard of hygiene than in much of the rest of the world, residents harbor a fear that public toilets are dark, dirty, smelly and scary.
  To cure the public’s phobia, the non-profit Nippon Foundation launched “The Tokyo Toilet Project,” tasking 16 well-known architects to renovate 17 public toilets located in the public parks of Shibuya, one of the busiest commercial areas of Tokyo.
     The mission was to apply innovative design to make public bathrooms accessible for everyone regardless of gender, age or disability, with a goal “that people will feel comfortable using these public toilets and to foster a spirit of hospitality for the next person,”  according to a statement from The Nippon Foundation.
  So far, the most talked-about design comes from Pritzker Prize-winning architect Shigeru Ban, whose transparent restrooms popped up this month in Haru-no-Ogawa Community Park and the Yoyogi Fukamachi Mini Park.
    The two units each have three cubicles, which are surrounded by transparent tinted glass in cyan, lime green, blue, yellow, pink or purple. The see-through design has a practical reason, which is to allow a person to easily check inside before entering.
    “There are two concerns with public toilets, especially those located in parks,” explains The Nippon Foundation, the non-profit organization. “The first is whether it is clean inside, and the second is that no one is secretly waiting inside.”
  The design relies on a new smartglass technology that turns the walls opaque when the door is locked. “At night, they light up the parks like a beautiful lantern,” according to The Nippon Foundation.
  Along with the two facilities designed by Ban, “The Tokyo Toilet Project” has also opened three other public restrooms, created by interior designer Masamichi Katayama in Ebisu Park; Pritzker winner Fumihiko Maki in Ebisu East Park; and New York-based furniture designer Nao Tamura near Ebisu Station.
  In the coming weeks, restrooms will open from architect Takenosuke Sakakura in Nishihara Itchome Park and Tadao Ando, yet another Pritzker Prize winner, in Jingu-Dori Park. The remainder of the project’s renovations are slated to open in the spring of 2021.
title: 为什么东京新建的透明公厕是天才的一击
title_en: Why Tokyo’s New Transparent Public Restrooms Are a Stroke of Genius

一个多月前，一部名为“利班”(Liban)的法国纪录片严厉批评了黎巴嫩社会和当局对待移民家政工人的方式。但许多黎巴嫩人非但没有对他们同胞的行为感到愤怒，反而对这位敢于玷污他们在法国声誉的电影制作人表示愤怒。一个团体甚至组织请愿书，反对这部纪录片在黎巴嫩脸书上掀起的最新热潮。
在这部纪录片之后，黎巴嫩小报Al-Jarass杂志决定通过一篇题为“不要在没有拐杖的情况下购买奴隶”的文章来捍卫黎巴嫩的荣誉。这篇文章混杂着廉价和种族主义的概括，指责外国女佣是小偷，甚至暗示对她们使用暴力是正当的。Al-Jarass杂志通常只刊登有抱负的流行艺术家的事迹。幸运的是，一些黎巴嫩非政府组织和记者公开回应了这篇文章，以示抗议。
争论可能有助于将注意力集中在某个话题上。在这种情况下，他们肯定帮助搅动了局面。但我们需要超越媒体的争议，承认潜在的愤怒：黎巴嫩对移民家政工人的严重虐待，这个国家已经将如此多的移民送往世界各地，而我们作为黎巴嫩人未能结束这种情况。
两周前，人权观察(Human Rights Watch)发布了一份报告，基于对100名斯里兰卡移民家政工人的采访，这些人在中东工作后返回斯里兰卡，其中包括许多在黎巴嫩的人。他们讲述了一个令人沮丧的故事，他们的雇主虐待他们，黎巴嫩当局方面官方漠不关心。
黎巴嫩雇主通常没收移徙家庭佣工的护照，并将他们限制在一天24小时的工作场所。在许多情况下，雇主拒绝允许他们给亲戚或朋友打电话。一些雇主还一次扣发工资数月至数年。
我没有离开的自由，无论白天黑夜，Krishnan说，她是一名42岁的斯里兰卡妇女，曾在黎巴嫩做过女佣。当他们出去的时候，他们锁上了房子并带走了钥匙，而我不得不呆在家里。
对于任何生活在黎巴嫩的人来说，这个消息都不会令人惊讶。令人惊讶的是，这么多黎巴嫩人认为他们有权保留雇员的护照。根据黎巴嫩明爱2005年的一项电话调查，接受采访的黎巴嫩雇主中有91%声称他们有权保留家政工人的护照。事实上，在某些情况下，雇主故意没收护照，违背工人的意愿扣留他们，实际上是强迫他们的女佣劳动。
其他斯里兰卡女佣描述了她们如何被迫在黎巴嫩长时间工作，有时在多个家庭工作。有些雇主甚至对女佣进行身体或性虐待。
然而，当农民工寻求正义时，他们面临的制度是，指控雇主的暴力行为意味着他们可能面临盗窃的反指控。这是一名26岁的斯里兰卡女佣的案例，她抱怨说，她被欠了2600美元的两年无薪工作。她的黎巴嫩雇主在她受雇的最后一天诬告她偷窃。她被监禁了两年，最后被判无罪。
对于那些认为我们的国家比邻国更好而寻求安慰的黎巴嫩人来说，这项研究并没有带来好消息。黎巴嫩的移民家政工人面临的虐待与他们在沙特阿拉伯、科威特或阿拉伯联合酋长国面临的虐待相同。而黎巴嫩劳动法，就像这些海湾国家的法律一样，明确将家政工人排除在基本保护之外。说到尊重劳工权利，黎巴嫩很难有好伙伴。
为了先发制人，我们必须承认，并不是所有的黎巴嫩雇主都虐待他们的家政工人。许多工人喜欢他们在黎巴嫩的逗留，并设法存钱帮助他们的家人回家。然而，仅仅因为不是每个人都虐待家政工人，这并不意味着黎巴嫩人正在采取足够的措施来阻止那些这样做的人的做法。
黎巴嫩当局需要尽其所能，人权观察最近的一份报告概述了黎巴嫩履行其国际义务可以遵循的许多建议。但我们作为一个社会应该做得更多。如果我们看到雇主虐待家庭佣工，我们应该与他对质。如果我们听到家政工人在邻居的门后尖叫，我们应该报警。如果我们不想因为我们对待家政工人的方式而受到社会的羞辱，我们就需要直面侵犯家政工人权利的黎巴嫩雇主。我们需要向世界表明，我们认为这种虐待是不可接受的。
几周前的一件事给了我希望，也许还没有失去一切。一名黎巴嫩妇女打电话给我们的办公室，报告一家机构，她怀疑该机构违背家政工人的意愿扣留了他们。遗憾的是，当局没有采取行动。但这位女士的手势表明，有些事情正在激动人心。这样的公民行为需要成倍增加。
在此之前，我们都为继续虐待为我们工作的移民而感到羞耻。

note_en:
Over a month ago, a French documentary, "Liban, Pays des Esclaves," harshly criticized Lebanese society and the authorities for their treatment of migrant domestic workers. But instead of being outraged by the behavior of their fellow citizens, many Lebanese expressed outrage against the filmmaker who dared to sully their reputation in France. One group even organized a petition against the documentary on Facebook, Lebanon's latest craze.
 Following the documentary, Al-Jarass magazine, a Lebanese tabloid that usually limits its pages to the exploits of aspiring pop artists, decided to defend Lebanon's honor through an article entitled, "Don't buy a slave without a stick." A jumble of cheap and racist generalizations, the article accused foreign maids of being thieves and even suggested that the use of violence against them was justified. Luckily, a number of Lebanese NGOs and journalists publicly responded to the article in protest.
 Controversies can be useful in focusing attention on a certain topic. And in this case, they surely helped to stir the pot. But we need to move beyond the media controversy and acknowledge the underlying outrage: the serious mistreatment of migrant domestic workers in Lebanon, a nation that has sent so many migrants across the world, and our failure as Lebanese to put an end to it.
 Two weeks ago, Human Rights Watch released a  report based on interviews with 100 Sri Lankan migrant domestic workers who had returned to Sri Lanka after working in the Middle East, including many in Lebanon. They tell a disheartening tale of ill-treatment by their employers and official indifference on the part of the Lebanese authorities.
 Lebanese employers routinely confiscated the passports of the migrant domestic workers and confined them to the workplace 24 hours a day. In many cases employers refused to allow them to telephone their relatives or friends. Some employers also withheld wages for months to years at a time.
 "I had no freedom to leave, day or night," said Krishnan, a 42-year-old Sri Lankan woman who had worked as a maid in Lebanon. "When they went out, they locked the house and took the key with them, and I had to stay inside."
 For anyone living in Lebanon, this news won't come as a surprise. What may be surprising is the fact that so many Lebanese think that they have the right to retain an employee's passport. According to a 2005 telephone survey by Caritas Lebanon, 91 percent of Lebanese employers interviewed claimed that they had the right to retain a domestic worker's passport. In fact, in some cases employers have intentionally confiscated passports to hold workers against their will, effectively subjecting their maids to forced labor.
 Other Sri Lankan maids described how they were forced to work very long hours in Lebanon, sometimes in multiple homes. Some employers even subject their maids to physical or sexual abuse.
 However, when migrant workers seek justice, they face a system where accusing their employer of violence means that they are likely to face counter-accusations of theft. This was the case of a 26-year-old Sri Lankan maid who complained she was owed $2,600 for two years of unpaid work. Her Lebanese employer falsely accused her of theft on her last day of employment. She was imprisoned for two years before finally being acquitted of the crime.
 For Lebanese who seek solace in the idea that our country is "better than its neighbors," the research does not hold good news. Migrant domestic workers in Lebanon face the same abuses that they face in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, or the United Arab Emirates. And Lebanese labor law, like the law in these Gulf countries, explicitly excludes domestic workers from its basic protections. When it comes to respect of labor rights, Lebanon is hardly in good company.
 To pre-empt some critics, we must acknowledge that not all Lebanon employers mistreat their domestic workers. Many workers enjoy their stay in Lebanon and manage to save money to help their families back home. However, just because not everyone mistreats domestic workers, this does not mean that Lebanese are doing enough to stop the practices of those who do.
 The Lebanese authorities need to do their part and a recent Human Rights Watch report outlines many recommendations that could be followed for Lebanon to meet its international obligations. But we as a society should do more. If we see that an employer mistreats a domestic worker, we should confront him about it. If we hear a domestic worker screaming behind a neighbor's door, we should call the police. If we don't want to be shamed as a society for our treatment of domestic workers, we need to confront Lebanese employers who abuse the rights of domestic workers. And we need to make clear to the world that we don't think such abuse is acceptable.
 An incident a couple of weeks ago gave me hope that maybe not all is lost yet. A Lebanese woman called our office to report an agency that she suspected was holding domestic workers against their will. Sadly, the authorities failed to act. But this woman's gesture was an indication that something is stirring. Such acts of citizenry need to multiply.
 Until then, we all share in the shame of the continuing abusive practices committed against migrants who came to work for us.

我们不会记得我们在大流行中做了什么|免费阅读。
这些天当我心不在焉的时候，我注意到它会飘荡到奇怪的地方--也就是遥远的酒店房间。苏黎世，去年夏末：酒店在轨道的错误一侧，但房间的两面墙上都有大窗户。几年前的达拉斯：酒店有一个巨大的中庭，里面有一条铁路模型；我一边听着关于后起之秀作曲家利奥·罗伯茨·扬纳维克(LeošJanáčEk)的播客，一边熨衬衫。兰乔幻影，一月：房间阳光明媚，离接待处很远；沙漠棕榈树旁的游泳池看起来很诱人，但很冷，到处都是树叶。
为什么，在所有的事情中，我的脑海里会跳跃到遥远的酒店呢？不是因为那里发生了很多事情：可悲的是，我必须承认，我从来没有在酒店房间里做过很多有趣的事情。显然，我的记忆是以一种奇特的方式工作的。
去年春天，我从日本度过了一生难忘的假期回来，并反思了它所产生的丰富记忆。当我在那里的时候，时间过得很快，但事后看来，在一个鲜活的新地方，10天的时间比在家里的10周产生了更多的记忆。
我把这种效果比作电影的压缩效果。视频压缩算法不是单独存储每一帧，而是从场景的第一帧开始，然后存储一系列“差异”-从一帧到下一帧的变化。一部有长镜头和固定摄像机的缓慢、沉思的电影可以比一部快速移动的动作片更容易压缩。
同样，回想起来，充满新体验的一周会显得更长。重复同样的程序一个月可能看起来没完没了，但在记忆中几乎不会是昙花一现：“差异”还不够明显，大脑不会为之烦恼。
在家工作了几个月后，我现在意识到这个账户有些不完整。新的经历确实对种植丰富的记忆很重要。但是，就其本身而言，这是不够的。如果我们的大脑想要集中注意力，一个新的物理空间似乎很重要。
新冠肺炎的禁闭，毕竟充满了新的体验。有些情况很严峻：我在疾病中失去了一位朋友；我在一次事故中摔伤了脸；我们不得不戴口罩，避免身体接触，担心下一卷卫生纸是从哪里来的。有些则更为积极：发现新的乐趣，磨练新的技能，克服新的挑战。
但我怀疑我是唯一一个发现自己对禁闭几个月的记忆相当薄弱的人。不管你在Zoom或Skype上和多少新朋友或老朋友聊天，他们都会开始一起诽谤，因为物理环境很单调：对话发生在一个人坐在同一张椅子上，在同一个房间里，盯着同一个电脑屏幕。
心理学家芭芭拉·特沃斯基(Barbara Tversky)是“运动中的心灵”(The Mind In Motion)一书的作者，她认为，我们的大脑是建立在对地点、空间和运动的认知基础上的。随着诸如“建立在基础上的”和“悄悄进入”这样的短语悄悄进入我们的语言。我们的大脑从帮助我们处理周围环境及其带来的威胁和机会开始。抽象思维是对这些基本空间能力的适应。
这可能就是为什么不是所有的新奇事物都是一样的。我们的大脑似乎特别生动地记录了一个新的地方。在听播客的同时熨烫衬衫没有什么特别新奇的，达拉斯酒店的房间也没有什么特别有趣的。但这是一个我以前从未去过的物理空间。这足以让我的大脑做笔记。
即使某个时刻与地点无关，而完全与智力或情感上的新鲜感有关，地点仍然会引起注意。当我想到我生命中重要的智力或情感关头时-从我第一次理解阿罗的不可能定理到我第一次遇到我的未婚妻-即使在那时，这些记忆也与物理位置联系在一起。
难怪记忆冠军经常使用“轨迹法”或“记忆宫殿”技术，通过在清晰的空间(比如童年时期的房间)描绘生动的图像来记忆长长的抽象信息列表。
我开始重新认识到前Covid时代习惯的价值：寻找新的阅读和写作场所，甚至是像新咖啡馆或新图书馆这样简单的地方。当一个人在不同的地方工作时，会有更新鲜的想法和更清晰的记忆：在一个新的地方，头脑会更警觉。
这可能就是为什么，当我们要求人们回忆历史上的关键时刻，如柏林墙倒塌或曼哈顿9/11恐怖袭击时，我们会问“当你听到这个消息时，你在哪里？”
新冠肺炎可能和任何一个插曲一样重要，但它不会引发同样尖锐的记忆。大流行期间你在哪里？在家。好几个月了。如果没有场景的物理变化，即使是新的体验也开始看起来都是一样的。
“你需要多出去走走，”有人曾经告诫我。她是对的。这些天来，我们都是这样做的。
note_en:
We won’t remember much of what we did in the pandemic | Free to read
 When my mind wanders these days, I’ve noticed that it wanders to odd places — namely, far-off hotel rooms. Zurich, late last summer: the hotel was on the wrong side of the tracks but the room had big windows on two walls. Dallas, a few years back: the hotel had a huge atrium with a model railway; I ironed my shirt while listening to a podcast about the late-blooming composer Leoš Janáček. Rancho Mirage, January: the room was a sunny stroll away from reception; the pool looked tempting beside the desert palms, but was cold and full of leaves.
 Why, of all things, would my mind jump to distant hotels? Not because anything much happened there: sadly, I must confess never to having done much of interest in a hotel room. Evidently, my memory is working in curious ways.
 Last spring, I returned from the  holiday of a lifetime in Japan, and reflected on the richness of the memories it had generated. Time flew by while I was there, but in hindsight 10 days somewhere vividly new had produced more memories than 10 weeks back home.
  I likened the effect to the compression of a film. Instead of storing each frame separately, video compression algorithms will start with the first frame of a scene and then store a series of “diffs” — changes from one frame to the next. A slow, contemplative movie with long scenes and fixed cameras can be compressed more than a fast-moving action flick.
 Similarly, a week full of new experiences will seem longer in retrospect. A month of repeating the same routine might seem endless, but will be barely a blip in the memory: the “diffs” are not significant enough for the brain to bother with.
 After months of working from home, I now realise that there was something incomplete about this account. New experiences are indeed important for planting a rich crop of memories. But, by itself, that is not enough. A new physical space seems to be important if our brains are to pay attention.
 The Covid-19 lockdown, after all, was full of new experiences. Some were grim: I lost a friend to the disease; I smashed my face up  in an accident; we had to wear masks and avoid physical contact and worry about where the next roll of toilet paper was coming from. Some were more positive: the discovery of new pleasures, the honing of new skills, the overcoming of new challenges.
 But I doubt I am alone in finding that my memory of the lockdown months is rather thin. No matter how many new people or old friends you talk to on Zoom or Skype, they all start to smear together because the physical context is monotonous: the conversations take place while one sits in the same chair, in the same room, staring at the same computer screen.
 The psychologist Barbara Tversky, author of  Mind in Motion, argues that our minds are built on a foundation of cognition about place, space and movement. That creeps into our language with phrases such as “built on a foundation” and “creeps into”. Our brains started by helping us process our surroundings and the threats and opportunities they presented. Abstract thinking is an adaptation of those basic spatial capacities.
  This may be why not all novelty is created alike. Our brains seem to record a new place with a particular vividness. There was nothing especially novel about ironing a shirt while listening to a podcast, and nothing especially intriguing about that Dallas hotel room. But it was a physical space in which I had never been before. That was enough to set my brain taking notes.
 Even when a moment has nothing to do with place and everything to do with intellectual or emotional novelty, place still registers. When I think about important intellectual or emotional junctures in my life — from the first time I understood  Arrow’s impossibility theorem to the first time I met my wife-to-be — even then, those memories are tied to a physical location.
 No wonder champion mnemonists often use the “method of loci” or the “memory palace” technique, memorising long lists of abstract information by picturing vivid images in well-defined spaces, such as the rooms of a childhood home.
 I’ve come to realise with renewed force the value of a pre-Covid habit: seeking out new places in which to read and to write, even something as simple as a new café or a new library. Fresher ideas and clearer memories come when one works somewhere different: in a new place, the mind is more alert.
 This may be why, when we ask people to recall pivotal moments in history such as the fall of the Berlin Wall or the 9/11 terror attacks in Manhattan, we ask “where were you when you heard?”
 Covid-19 may be as significant an episode as any, but it will not trigger the same sharp memories. Where were you during the pandemic? At home. For months. And without a physical change of scene, even new experiences all start to seem the same.
 “You need to get out more,” someone once admonished me. She was right. These days, we all do.
title: “我们不会记得我们在大流行期间做了什么。”
title_en: 'We Won't Remember Much of What We Did in the Pandemic'
